findings.description: yaml.load() is called without a SafeLoader, allowing arbitrary Python object instantiation and remote code execution from a crafted config file; must use yaml.safe_load.
Precedence is inverted versus the documented contract: env overlay is applied before the file, so the file overrides env (later update() wins), making 'environment variables > config file' false.
get() calls load() which re-reads and re-parses the YAML file and re-scans os.environ on every single-key access, so require/feature_enabled trigger a full reload each call with no caching.

_read_file has no try/except: malformed YAML raises yaml.YAMLError and open() can raise PermissionError/IsADirectoryError even after the os.path.exists check, crashing instead of degrading gracefully like the missing-file path.

_coerce greedily converts any all-digit or float-like env string to int/float, corrupting values that must stay strings (zip codes, versions, numeric passwords) and accepting inf/nan and whitespace-padded numbers.
feature_enabled assumes feature_flags is a dict, but an env override or a YAML scalar makes it a string/int, so flags.get raises AttributeError.
require treats a key whose configured value is None (e.g. YAML null) as missing and raises KeyError, unable to distinguish an absent key from a present null value.
Env overlay keys are flat and replace whole values, so nested config like feature_flags can never be deep-merged from the environment and double-underscore/nested intents are lost.
_coerce attempts int() then float() parsing inside try/except for every env var on every load; minor but avoidable given load() runs per get().
